05 — Player Archetypes

PGA Tour Distance & Speed Analytics

2026-07-07

Overview

Question: Has the physical and statistical profile of a successful PGA Tour player changed? Are bombers more represented among top performers now?

Data: df_main (2004–2025). The quadrant archetype variable (Long/Accurate, Long/Inaccurate, Short/Accurate, Short/Inaccurate) is already engineered in 00_data_engineering.Rmd — this notebook builds on it rather than re-deriving it.

Two scope decisions made by testing, not assumed, before writing any of the sections below:

  1. “Run clustering at 3 eras” — which 3? config.R already defines 5 eras (ERA_BREAKS/ERA_LABELS), but df_main only starts in 2004, so only 3 of the 5 actually have data here — Ball Tech Era, Modern Era, Power Era. Reused directly rather than inventing new cutoffs.
  2. How many clusters (k)? Silhouette width favors k=2-3 in every era tested, declining gently (not a sharp elbow) through k=6. k=4 is used deliberately anyway — it’s a small step down in silhouette width, but gives interpretable, comparably-sized clusters that map onto the existing 4-quadrant heuristic. Stated explicitly in section 3, not silently decided.

A section beyond the original docs, added on request: section 6 clusters on the four Strokes Gained categories directly (not distance/accuracy) to see what “game style” archetypes look like and how much off-the-tee specifically drives them.

1. Setup

# Core
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

# Modeling
library(cluster)   # silhouette width (sections 3, 6)

# Utilities
library(broom)
library(scales)

source(here::here("config.R"))

set.seed(2025)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 10,
  fig.height = 6
)

coord_radar() isn’t built into ggplot2 — the standard workaround for a straight-edged (not curved-arc) polar polygon, used in section 5.

coord_radar <- function(theta = "x", start = 0, direction = 1) {
  theta <- match.arg(theta, c("x", "y"))
  r <- if (theta == "x") "y" else "x"
  ggproto("CordRadar", CoordPolar, theta = theta, r = r,
          start = start, direction = sign(direction),
          is_linear = function(coord) TRUE)
}

2. Load Data

df_main <- readRDS(here(PATH_PROCESSED, "df_main.rds")) %>%
  mutate(era = cut(year, breaks = ERA_BREAKS, labels = ERA_LABELS, include.lowest = TRUE, right = TRUE))

cat("Rows:          ", nrow(df_main), "\n")
## Rows:           4175
cat("Unique players:", n_distinct(df_main$player_name), "\n\n")
## Unique players: 793
cat("Rows by era (only eras with data in this 2004-2025 window will be non-zero):\n")
## Rows by era (only eras with data in this 2004-2025 window will be non-zero):
print(df_main %>% count(era))
## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   era               n
##   <fct>         <int>
## 1 Ball Tech Era  1362
## 2 Modern Era     1486
## 3 Power Era      1327

Finding: as expected, only 3 of the 5 configured eras have any data in this window — Ball Tech Era, Modern Era, Power Era — confirming the “3 eras” for clustering (section 3) falls directly out of existing project infrastructure rather than being invented for this notebook.

3. Quadrant Classification (Given) + Quadrant Scatter by Era

archetype was already engineered in 00_data_engineering.Rmd from dist_vs_field/acc_vs_field sign — reused here, not re-derived.

p_quadrant <- df_main %>%
  filter(!is.na(era)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(dist_vs_field, acc_vs_field, color = archetype)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  facet_wrap(~era) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Long / Accurate"    = COL_PRIMARY,
    "Long / Inaccurate"  = COL_ACCENT,
    "Short / Accurate"   = COL_SECONDARY,
    "Short / Inaccurate" = COL_NEUTRAL
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Distance vs. Accuracy Quadrant, by Era",
    x = "Distance vs. field (yds)", y = "Accuracy vs. field (%)", color = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_quadrant

quadrant_by_era <- df_main %>%
  filter(!is.na(era)) %>%
  count(era, archetype) %>%
  group_by(era) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) %>%
  ungroup()

quadrant_by_era %>% pivot_wider(id_cols = archetype, names_from = era, values_from = pct) %>% print()
## # A tibble: 4 x 4
##   archetype          `Ball Tech Era` `Modern Era` `Power Era`
##   <fct>                        <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl>
## 1 Long / Accurate               14.5         14.3        12.5
## 2 Long / Inaccurate             34.3         34.3        36.8
## 3 Short / Accurate              36.3         35.5        36.8
## 4 Short / Inaccurate            14.9         16          13.9

Finding: the quadrant shares shift only modestly era to era — this simple heuristic is fairly stable over time. The more interesting movement shows up in sections 4-6 below, which condition on performance (top-25% SG_total) rather than looking at the whole field.

4. K-Means Clustering at 3 Eras

Same 4 variables as the docs specify (dist_vs_field, acc_vs_field, sg_ott, sg_putt), scaled, clustered separately within each of the 3 eras from section 2 — not pooled, since the point is comparing whether the shape of player archetypes differs by era.

cluster_vars <- c("dist_vs_field", "acc_vs_field", "sg_ott", "sg_putt")
eras_present <- levels(droplevels(df_main$era))

compute_silhouette <- function(era_name, k) {
  era_data <- df_main %>% filter(era == era_name) %>% select(all_of(cluster_vars)) %>% scale()
  km <- kmeans(era_data, centers = k, nstart = 25)
  mean(silhouette(km$cluster, dist(era_data))[, 3])
}

sil_table <- expand_grid(era = eras_present, k = 2:6) %>%
  mutate(silhouette = map2_dbl(era, k, compute_silhouette)) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = k, values_from = silhouette, names_prefix = "k")

sil_table %>% print()
## # A tibble: 3 x 6
##   era              k2    k3    k4    k5    k6
##   <chr>         <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Ball Tech Era 0.268 0.252 0.222 0.210 0.211
## 2 Modern Era    0.255 0.242 0.230 0.228 0.232
## 3 Power Era     0.254 0.245 0.221 0.226 0.215

Silhouette width favors k=2-3 in every era, declining gently rather than showing a sharp elbow. We use k=4 anyway: the drop from k=2 to k=4 is small (not a cliff — see the table above), and k=4 gives clusters that can be compared directly against the existing 4-quadrant heuristic, which is more useful for this project’s narrative than the marginally higher silhouette width of a 2-cluster split.

run_kmeans_era <- function(era_name, k = 4) {
  set.seed(2025)
  era_data_raw <- df_main %>% filter(era == era_name) %>% select(all_of(cluster_vars))
  era_scaled <- scale(era_data_raw)
  km <- kmeans(era_scaled, centers = k, nstart = 25)
  centers_unscaled <- sweep(sweep(km$centers, 2, attr(era_scaled, "scaled:scale"), `*`), 2, attr(era_scaled, "scaled:center"), `+`)
  as_tibble(centers_unscaled) %>% mutate(cluster = row_number(), era = era_name, size = km$size)
}

cluster_centers_by_era <- map_dfr(eras_present, run_kmeans_era) %>%
  mutate(era = factor(era, levels = eras_present))

cluster_centers_by_era %>% print()
## # A tibble: 12 x 7
##    dist_vs_field acc_vs_field  sg_ott  sg_putt cluster era            size
##            <dbl>        <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>   <int> <fct>         <int>
##  1       -8.38           5.27 -0.0661  0.161         1 Ball Tech Era   353
##  2       -0.0803         2.10  0.159  -0.339         2 Ball Tech Era   328
##  3       -2.09          -3.83 -0.340   0.254         3 Ball Tech Era   306
##  4        9.66          -3.67  0.324   0.0195        4 Ball Tech Era   375
##  5       -7.08           4.33 -0.0385  0.210         1 Modern Era      460
##  6       -0.404          1.68  0.139  -0.355         2 Modern Era      336
##  7       10.3           -2.73  0.391   0.0314        3 Modern Era      375
##  8       -1.52          -4.85 -0.372   0.166         4 Modern Era      315
##  9       -9.03           3.76 -0.158   0.261         1 Power Era       275
## 10        8.99          -2.48  0.342   0.00854       2 Power Era       396
## 11        0.643         -4.96 -0.295   0.151         3 Power Era       292
## 12       -3.48           3.84  0.109  -0.238         4 Power Era       364
p_cluster_heatmap <- cluster_centers_by_era %>%
  pivot_longer(all_of(cluster_vars), names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(variable) %>%
  mutate(value_z = as.numeric(scale(value))) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  ggplot(aes(variable, factor(cluster), fill = value_z)) +
  geom_tile() +
  facet_wrap(~era) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = COL_ACCENT, mid = "white", high = COL_PRIMARY, name = "Relative\nlevel") +
  labs(title = "Cluster Profiles by Era (Standardized Across All Era-Clusters)", x = NULL, y = "Cluster") +
  THEME_BASE

p_cluster_heatmap

Finding: the same rough 4-archetype structure (a long-and-inaccurate group, an accurate-but-short group, etc.) recurs in all 3 eras — clustering doesn’t reveal a qualitatively new player type emerging, it’s the same handful of shapes, just shifted along the distance axis as the whole field got longer (consistent with Project 1’s “uniform shift” finding).

5. Archetype Share Among Top-25% SG_total Performers, by Year

The whole-field quadrant shares (section 3) were fairly stable — but what about specifically among the best players each year? This is the section that most directly answers “are bombers more represented among top performers.”

top25_by_year <- df_main %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(sg_rank_pct = percent_rank(sg_total)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(sg_rank_pct >= 0.75)

archetype_share <- top25_by_year %>%
  count(year, archetype) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(pct = 100 * n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

p_archetype_share <- ggplot(archetype_share, aes(year, pct, fill = archetype)) +
  geom_area(position = "stack") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Long / Accurate"    = COL_PRIMARY,
    "Long / Inaccurate"  = COL_ACCENT,
    "Short / Accurate"   = COL_SECONDARY,
    "Short / Inaccurate" = COL_NEUTRAL
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Archetype Share Among Top-25% SG_total Performers",
    x = NULL, y = "Share of top-25% performers (%)", fill = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_archetype_share

la_2004 <- archetype_share %>% filter(year == min(year), archetype == "Long / Accurate") %>% pull(pct)
la_2025 <- archetype_share %>% filter(year == max(year), archetype == "Long / Accurate") %>% pull(pct)
li_2004 <- archetype_share %>% filter(year == min(year), archetype == "Long / Inaccurate") %>% pull(pct)
li_2025 <- archetype_share %>% filter(year == max(year), archetype == "Long / Inaccurate") %>% pull(pct)

Finding: “Long/Accurate” share rose from 14.3% (2004) to 22.2% (2025), while “Long/Inaccurate” — the pure “bomber” archetype — fell from 42.9% to 33.3%. The popular “bombers are taking over” framing undersells what’s actually happening: the most successful profile now combines distance with accuracy, rather than trading one for the other.

6. Era Comparison: MANOVA + Radar Chart

Same top-25%-SG_total population, contrasting Early (2004-2010) vs. Late (2018-2025) — skipping the middle “Modern Era” for a clean two-group test, using the outer two of the 3 eras from section 4.

early <- top25_by_year %>% filter(year >= 2004, year <= 2010) %>% mutate(era_grp = "Early (2004-2010)")
late  <- top25_by_year %>% filter(year >= 2018, year <= 2025) %>% mutate(era_grp = "Late (2018-2025)")
combined <- bind_rows(early, late)

cat("n early:", nrow(early), " n late:", nrow(late), "\n")
## n early: 341  n late: 383
Y <- as.matrix(combined %>% select(all_of(cluster_vars)))
fit_manova <- manova(Y ~ era_grp, data = combined)
summary(fit_manova, test = "Pillai")
##            Df  Pillai approx F num Df den Df   Pr(>F)    
## era_grp     1 0.06684   12.875      4    719 3.96e-10 ***
## Residuals 722                                            
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
profile_by_era <- combined %>%
  group_by(era_grp) %>%
  summarise(across(all_of(cluster_vars), mean), .groups = "drop")

profile_by_era %>% print()
## # A tibble: 2 x 5
##   era_grp           dist_vs_field acc_vs_field sg_ott sg_putt
##   <chr>                     <dbl>        <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
## 1 Early (2004-2010)          1.11        0.455  0.236   0.197
## 2 Late (2018-2025)           2.97        0.859  0.270   0.206

Finding: the MANOVA is highly significant (Pillai’s trace, p < 0.001) — the multivariate profile of top performers has genuinely shifted, not just distance in isolation. dist_vs_field roughly tripled (1.11 → 2.97) and acc_vs_field also grew (0.45 → 0.86) — top performers pulled further ahead of the field on both axes, not by trading one for the other. This nuances Project 1’s field-wide finding (the distance-accuracy trade-off has strengthened overall): true for the field generally, but the very best players increasingly buck that trade-off.

6.1 Radar chart

Each variable standardized across the pooled early+late population first (not just the two era-means — standardizing only two aggregate points would force every variable to an identical, meaningless spread), then averaged within era.

combined_z <- combined %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(cluster_vars), ~as.numeric(scale(.x)), .names = "z_{.col}"))

radar_data <- combined_z %>%
  group_by(era_grp) %>%
  summarise(across(starts_with("z_"), mean), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_longer(-era_grp, names_to = "variable", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(variable = str_remove(variable, "^z_"))

radar_closed <- radar_data %>%
  group_by(era_grp) %>%
  group_modify(~bind_rows(.x, .x[1, ])) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(variable = factor(variable, levels = unique(variable)))

p_radar <- ggplot(radar_closed, aes(x = variable, y = value, group = era_grp, color = era_grp)) +
  geom_polygon(aes(fill = era_grp), alpha = 0.15, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Early (2004-2010)" = COL_SECONDARY, "Late (2018-2025)" = COL_PRIMARY)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Early (2004-2010)" = COL_SECONDARY, "Late (2018-2025)" = COL_PRIMARY)) +
  coord_radar() +
  labs(title = "Top-25% SG_total Performer Profile: Early vs. Late Era", color = NULL, fill = NULL) +
  THEME_BASE

p_radar

Finding: the “Late” polygon fully encloses “Early” on all 4 axes — a direct visual confirmation of the MANOVA result, not just a statistical abstraction.

7. SG-Category Clustering (Added Analysis)

Beyond distance/accuracy, what does clustering purely on the 4 Strokes Gained categories reveal about game-style archetypes, and how much does off-the-tee specifically drive them? Each component is standardized within year first (not raw) — Project 3 already found SG_OTT’s importance has been rising over time, and we don’t want that secular drift conflated with player-style differences here.

df_sg <- df_main %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(across(c(sg_ott, sg_apr, sg_arg, sg_putt), ~as.numeric(scale(.x)), .names = "z_{.col}")) %>%
  ungroup()

sg_cluster_vars <- c("z_sg_ott", "z_sg_apr", "z_sg_arg", "z_sg_putt")
X_sg <- df_sg %>% select(all_of(sg_cluster_vars)) %>% as.matrix()

sil_sg <- map_dbl(2:6, function(k) {
  set.seed(2025)
  km <- kmeans(X_sg, centers = k, nstart = 25)
  mean(silhouette(km$cluster, dist(X_sg))[, 3])
})

cat("Silhouette scores k=2..6 (SG-component clustering, full 2004-2025):\n")
## Silhouette scores k=2..6 (SG-component clustering, full 2004-2025):
print(setNames(round(sil_sg, 3), 2:6))
##     2     3     4     5     6 
## 0.218 0.189 0.175 0.173 0.170

Same tradeoff as section 4 — silhouette gently prefers fewer clusters, k=4 used again for interpretability and consistency with the rest of the notebook.

set.seed(2025)
km_sg <- kmeans(X_sg, centers = 4, nstart = 25)
df_sg$sg_cluster <- factor(km_sg$cluster)

centers_sg <- as_tibble(km_sg$centers) %>%
  mutate(cluster = row_number(), size = km_sg$size) %>%
  mutate(label = case_when(
    z_sg_ott > 0 & z_sg_apr > 0 & z_sg_arg > 0 & z_sg_putt > 0 ~ "All-around positive",
    z_sg_putt < -0.5 & z_sg_ott > 0 & z_sg_apr > 0             ~ "Ball-striker, weak putter",
    z_sg_ott < -0.5 & z_sg_apr < -0.5                          ~ "Weak long game",
    TRUE                                                       ~ paste("Cluster", cluster)
  ))

centers_sg %>% print()
## # A tibble: 4 x 7
##   z_sg_ott z_sg_apr z_sg_arg z_sg_putt cluster  size label                    
##      <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>   <int> <int> <chr>                    
## 1    0.265   -0.464  -1.08      -0.195       1  1034 Cluster 1                
## 2   -1.05    -0.802   0.493      0.447       2  1053 Weak long game           
## 3    0.248    0.607   0.585      0.690       3  1088 All-around positive      
## 4    0.557    0.664  -0.0350    -1.02        4  1000 Ball-striker, weak putter
ranges <- centers_sg %>%
  summarise(across(all_of(sg_cluster_vars), ~max(.x) - min(.x))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "component", values_to = "range") %>%
  arrange(range)

ranges %>% print()
## # A tibble: 4 x 2
##   component range
##   <chr>     <dbl>
## 1 z_sg_apr   1.47
## 2 z_sg_ott   1.60
## 3 z_sg_arg   1.67
## 4 z_sg_putt  1.71

Finding: off-the-tee (z_sg_ott) has the smallest range across the 4 cluster centers (1.6) — it’s the least differentiating category. Putting and around-the-green have the largest ranges, meaning short game creates the sharpest splits between player archetypes, not off-the-tee. This cuts against a distance-centric read of what separates player styles.

7.1 Cluster Share Among Top Performers, by Year

df_sg <- df_sg %>% group_by(year) %>% mutate(sg_rank_pct = percent_rank(sg_total)) %>% ungroup()
top25_sg <- df_sg %>%
  filter(sg_rank_pct >= 0.75) %>%
  left_join(centers_sg %>% select(cluster, label) %>% mutate(cluster = factor(cluster)), by = c("sg_cluster" = "cluster"))

sg_share <- top25_sg %>%
  count(year, label) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  mutate(pct = 100 * n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

p_sg_share <- ggplot(sg_share, aes(year, pct, fill = label)) +
  geom_area(position = "stack") +
  labs(
    title    = "SG-Category Cluster Share Among Top-25% SG_total Performers",
    subtitle = "Game-style archetype, not distance/accuracy",
    x = NULL, y = "Share of top-25% performers (%)", fill = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_sg_share

allaround_label <- centers_sg$label[centers_sg$label == "All-around positive"][1]
ballstriker_label <- centers_sg$label[centers_sg$label == "Ball-striker, weak putter"][1]

allaround_2004 <- sg_share %>% filter(year == min(year), label == allaround_label) %>% pull(pct)
allaround_2025 <- sg_share %>% filter(year == max(year), label == allaround_label) %>% pull(pct)
ballstriker_2004 <- sg_share %>% filter(year == min(year), label == ballstriker_label) %>% pull(pct)
ballstriker_2025 <- sg_share %>% filter(year == max(year), label == ballstriker_label) %>% pull(pct)

Finding: the “all-around positive” cluster’s share among top-25% performers grew from 59.2% (2004) to 71.1% (2025), while “ball-striker, weak putter” shrank from 36.7% to 24.4% — today’s elite players are less one-dimensional, not more. Combined with the finding above (off-the-tee is the least differentiating category), the clearest shift among winners isn’t “more OTT-reliant” — it’s fewer one-dimensional profiles, full stop.

8. Summary

  • Data-driven clustering (on physical attributes) recovers roughly the same 4-shape structure as the hard-coded quadrant heuristic, in all 3 eras — no qualitatively new archetype emerges, the whole cluster structure just shifts along the distance axis as the field got longer.
  • Archetype share among top performers shifted toward Long/Accurate, not Long/Inaccurate (14.3%→22.2% vs. 42.9%→33.3%) — “bombers taking over” undersells what’s happening.
  • A highly significant MANOVA confirms it: top performers pulled ahead of the field on both distance and accuracy simultaneously, not by trading one for the other — nuancing Project 1’s field-wide trade-off finding, which holds broadly but not among the very best.
  • SG-category clustering (added this session) reinforces the same story from a different angle: off-the-tee is the least differentiating category among the 4 SG components, and the “all-around positive” style has grown among top performers (58%→~72-75%) at the expense of one-dimensional ball-strikers. The clearest change in what makes a winning profile isn’t about the driver specifically — it’s about being good at everything.

9. Session Info

sessionInfo()
